sound_type: sharp crack + rumble cascade -- ice fracture impulse followed by sustained cascade reverberating through alpine valley terrain, E=6
ecosystem: Glacial front -- alpine terrain, moraine, high-relief Cascade valleys
season: Summer (peak calving season -- thermal loading, meltwater penetration, diurnal cycle)

key_glaciers.Carbon: Lowest elevation glacier in lower 48 (terminus ~3,500 ft). Deep valley, enclosed acoustic environment, pronounced echo character.
key_glaciers.Emmons: Largest glacier by area in lower 48 (~4.3 sq miles). Open northeast flank, more diffuse acoustic signature, lower reverb.

acoustic_physics.phase_1_crack: Ice fracture -- crystalline structure fails suddenly, releasing elastic strain energy as explosive mechanical impulse. Fracture propagates at high velocity. Sharp, impulsive, cannon-like at close range.
acoustic_physics.phase_2_impact: Separated ice mass falls onto glacier, moraine, or proglacial lake. Low-frequency thud initiates cascade.
acoustic_physics.phase_3_cascade: 10-60+ seconds of overlapping impacts, ice disintegration, valley reverberation. Duration depends on ice volume and terrain geometry.
acoustic_physics.terrain_amplification: High-relief Cascade valleys with steep walls create natural amplification and echo. Carbon Glacier's enclosed valley vs Emmons' open terrain: distinct signatures.
acoustic_physics.fundamental_frequency_crack_hz: 100-1000 Hz primary; high harmonic content up to several kHz for the initial fracture crack
acoustic_physics.fundamental_frequency_cascade_hz: 20-500 Hz for sustained rumble cascade -- low-frequency dominated

nodes.title: Sharp Crack -- Ice Fracture Mechanics
nodes.description: Crystalline ice fractures suddenly. Elastic strain energy releases as explosive mechanical impulse. Initial fracture crack propagates at high velocity through ice mass. The first and most impulsive component of the calving signature.

nodes.title: Rumble Cascade -- Multi-Phase Acoustic Decay
nodes.description: Post-fracture: fall, impact, disintegration, valley reverb. 10-60 seconds. Valley walls amplify. Carbon (enclosed) vs Emmons (open) produce distinct signatures. The long tail of the calving event.

nodes.title: 25 Named Glaciers -- Most Glaciated Peak in Lower 48
nodes.description: Mt Rainier carries 25 named glaciers covering ~35 square miles. Carbon Glacier (lowest elevation in lower 48, ~3,500 ft terminus). Emmons Glacier (largest by area, ~4.3 sq miles). More glacial ice than any other peak in contiguous US.

nodes.title: Summer Thermal Dynamics -- Peak Calving Season
nodes.description: Solar loading, meltwater penetration, basal flow lubrication. Diurnal cycle: morning quiet, afternoon peak calving frequency on sun-facing aspects. Summer resolves winter's accumulated mechanical stress as sound.

nodes.title: Geophysics Arc -- Mass Movement Trilogy (265-267)
nodes.description: Degrees 265-267: glacier calving (ice) → rockslide (rock) → mudslide (saturated soil). Three mass-movement mechanisms representing Cascade geomorphological dynamics. The engine documents the mountain range actively reshaping itself.

cross_references.relationship: Downstream consequence. Rainier's glaciers feed the White, Carbon, Nisqually rivers. The waterfalls in the engine are downstream from the glacial system documented here.
Preceding degree E=9 maximum contrasts with E=6 here. Glacier calving is lower energy intensity but vastly greater mass and spatial scale.
Mass-movement sequence continues. Rockslide is acoustically related: crack + cascade. Different material (rock vs ice), same gravitational energy release mechanism.
Pacific moisture feeds both thunderstorms and glaciers. Storm deposits become glacial ice become calving events. Upstream relationship.
